slides.title: The Infinite String Problem 🌌
slides.content:
Imagine you have a `User` with a `role: string`. That looks fine, right?

But what stops another developer from typing `role: "Supreme Overlord"`? A simple `string` type is infinitely vast. You need to restrict the exact values it can take.

slides.title: String Literal Unions
slides.content: The easiest way to restrict values is a Literal Union. You literally define the exact strings allowed, joined by a pipe `|`.

quizData.question: Can you use Literal Unions for numbers too?

options.text: No, Unions are strictly for strings.
Yes, but they must be negative numbers.

quizData.explanation: Literal Unions work with Strings, Numbers, and even Booleans! You securely restrict the valid inputs effortlessly.

slides.title: The Power of Enums 🏷️
slides.content:
While Unions are great, `Enums` (Enumerations) offer a more structured, object-like way to group named constants.

Unlike Unions, Enums actually exist as real objects at runtime!

quizData.question: You can assign explicit String values to Enums instead of letting TypeScript auto-assign numbers.
quizData.explanation: String Enums are incredibly popular! `enum LogLevel { Error = "ERROR", Warn = "WARN" }` makes debugging much easier because you see the raw string value.

slides.title: Which one to use? ⚖️
slides.content:
Use **Literal Unions** (`"admin" | "user"`) 90% of the time. They are lightweight, completely stripped out during compilation, and beautifully simple.

Use **Enums** when you have a massive list of deeply related constants and you want the IDE's dot-notation autocomplete to easily guide other developers.

slides.title: Quest Complete! 🛡️
slides.content: You just securely locked down your types. No more "Supreme Overlords" ruining the database! You've successfully completely mastered perfectly explicitly securely controlling the precisely perfectly effortlessly securely cleanly strictly appropriately intelligently brilliantly seamlessly dynamically gracefully explicitly identically flawlessly beautifully exactly safely correctly ideally values natively brilliantly!
